findings.title: Protocol-relative open redirect via trailing-slash path normalization
findings.description: handleAppRequest() (the production dispatcher returned by createRequestHandler / app.ts:76, SPEC §9.5) builds a 308 redirect directly from the normalized request pathname with no validation that the target is a safe same-origin path: `url.pathname = match.normalization.redirect.pathname; ... headers: { Location: `${url.pathname}${url.search}${url.hash}` }`. The pathname is taken straight from `new URL(request.url).pathname` and normalized by normalizePathname() in match.ts, which removes a trailing slash but does NOT collapse or reject leading double-slashes. Attack: a victim is lured to `https://victim.com//evil.com/` (path = `//evil.com/`, host still victim.com). normalizePathname strips the trailing slash, yielding redirect target `//evil.com`; because it differs from the input it returns a 308. app-request.ts then emits `Location: //evil.com`. A `//host` Location is a protocol-relative URL, so the browser navigates to `https://evil.com`. (Backslash variants like `/\evil.com/` are converted to `//evil.com/` by the URL parser and work identically.) This requires no auth, no CSRF token, and no session — purely a crafted link — enabling phishing, OAuth/redirect-flow token theft, and bypass of referer-based protections. Note: the trailing slash is what triggers the 308; `//evil.com` with no trailing slash does not redirect.
findings.recommendation: Before emitting the Location, guarantee the redirect target is a single-origin path: reject or collapse any pathname that begins with `//` (or `/\`). E.g. normalize leading slashes to a single `/` in normalizePathname, or in app-request.ts assert `redirect.pathname.startsWith('/') && !redirect.pathname.startsWith('//')` and 404/400 otherwise. Building the Location via `new URL(path, requestOrigin)` and re-extracting only pathname+search+hash would also neutralize the protocol-relative form.
